text_blocks.text: James Wilson said during the Philadelphia Convention
in 1787 that, "Laws may be unjust, may be unwise, maybe be
dangerous, may be destructive; and yet not be so
unconstitutional as to justify the Judges in refusing to give
them effect." George Mason agreed that judges "could
declare an unconstitutional law void. But with regard to
every law, however unjust, oppressive or pernicious, which did
not come plainly under this description, they would be
under the necessity as judges to give it a free course." Chief
Justice John Marshall (joined by Justice Joseph Story) took
a similar position in 1827: "When its existence as law is
denied, that existence cannot be proved by showing what are
